text: The surgeon inspects the entire course of the esophagus, visually assessing for any adhesions or signs of metastasis.
The surgeon begins dissection in the infra-azygous region, carefully separating the anterior aspect of the esophagus from the pericardium using robotic instruments.

The surgeon divides the pleura and carefully mobilizes lymph nodes and lymphatics towards the esophagus using robotic instruments. The goal is to remove at least fifteen lymph nodes for adequate staging.
The surgeon carefully removes a lymph node, positioning it towards the esophageal specimen for further examination.
The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament near the inferior pulmonary vein, continuing the dissection along the esophagus.
The surgeon mobilizes the fat surrounding the pericardium towards the specimen, with the pulmonary artery visible in the surgical field.
The assistant provides traction on the pericardium, facilitating the separation of fat, lymphatics, and lymph nodes away from the heart and pericardium.
The surgeon dissects down to the right crest near the diaphragmatic hiatus, carefully separating tissue in this area.
The surgeon dissects lymph nodes and fat near the pulmonary artery, then moves towards the subcarinal area to conclude the lymph node dissection.

The surgeon divides both vagus nerves below the carina. The subcarinal lymph node dissection results in minor oozing due to the vascularity and small vessels retracting after division. Bleeding is controlled with pressure or cauterization.

The surgeon dissects the esophagus away from the left bronchus, encountering the first bronchial artery. The pleura is divided between the azygous vein and the posterior part of the esophagus, with caution taken to avoid injuring the thoracic duct located between the azygous vein and the aorta. Visible fat in the area contains the thoracic duct.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the thoracic duct above the diaphragmatic hiatus, while some surgeons routinely ligate the duct and move all surrounding tissue towards the esophagus.

The surgeon performs an en bloc resection, removing the primary tumor along with the pericardium, thoracic duct, azygous vein, intercostal vessels, bilateral pleura, and surrounding cuff of crura, if adjacent.
The surgeon carefully dissects and mobilizes the fat and lymphatic lymph nodes towards the esophagectomy specimen, ensuring the thoracic duct remains intact and uninjured.

The surgeon divides the left side mediastinal pleura and retracts it towards the specimen to achieve a proper margin. The left lung is visible through the surgical window as the surgeon proceeds down towards the diaphragmatic hiatus on the left side.
The surgeon completes the dissection in the infra-azygous region and proceeds to dissect the supra-azygous region, focusing on the anatomical area above the azygous vein.

The surgeon identifies the tracheal ring impressions and begins dividing the pleura near the azygous vein. Care is taken to avoid using an active energy source near the tracheoesophageal membrane, instead applying traction to separate the pleura from the tracheal membrane.
The surgeon frees the arch of the azygous vein and places two Chenamom hemlock clips on either side before dividing the vein into two sections.

The surgeon dissects the area between the aorta and pulmonary vessel, moving towards the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and left paratracheal area. The assistant provides traction on the tracheal ring, allowing the surgeon to access both the ventral and dorsal sides of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection without using an energy source to carefully remove a chain of lymph nodes from the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on dissecting a large lymphatic structure, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon navigates through the anatomical plane with minimal bleeding, revealing the left recurrent laryngeal nerve curving around the reticule arch. A branch of the nerve leading to the esophagus is visibly divided.
The surgeon introduces umbilical tape to provide traction on the dissected esophagus, allowing the assistant to pull it and facilitate completion of the dissection on the left side.
The surgeon completes the subcarinal lymph node dissection, carefully removing the lymph nodes to prevent blood spoilage in the operative field. The assistant retrieves the excised lymph nodes.
The surgeon removes the umbilical tape from the dissected esophagus and inserts it on the left side of the trachea, near the aorta and carina.
